intent:
PREMISE CONTRACT (story 24; v3 — round-trip 2, addresses §E.7.r2 unmotivated friend arrival + closer-as-recitation; addresses §E.7.5.r2 over-marked s0 私は)
  one_sentence_event:    The narrator brings the book that holds yesterday's stone to school; when the book is opened on the desk the stone falls out; the friend already sitting at the next desk sees it and asks what and whose, and the narrator picks the stone up, shows it, and CLAIMS it as her own — using her mother's denial-words as the legal basis for repossession.
  change_of_state:       at s0 the stone is hidden inside the book in the bag; at the closer the stone is publicly seen, has changed hands rhetorically (mother's 'no one's' becomes narrator's '...so it's mine'), and is back in the book by the narrator's hand. Story 23 dispossessed the stone; story 24 RE-POSSESSES it.
  scene_novelty_claim:   I am NOT writing a garden, room, or study story; this is a classroom, fresh.
  anchor_novelty_claim:  Anchor is 本 (the book that carries the stone). Last appeared as a verb-object only; never as anchor across sentences.
  closer_shape_claim:    Closer shape: COMPOUND ACTION+DECLARATION (verb 入れる + quoted claim). NOT a noun-pile, NOT 「Nは Adj です」, NOT a mirror of stories 21/22/23. Closer is the moment the narrator FLIPS her mother's denial into her own claim of ownership — semantic inversion via reuse of identical surface.
  why_not_filler:        bag/desk earn s0 as the cargo's place. Book is the lid that opens. Stone falls because gravity (s2). Friend is already at the NEXT desk (s3 — 隣の友達 — pre-existing position, not teleport, not unmotivated arrival); friend's reaction to the stone falling is natural sight, not coincidence. s4 question is an authentic 'what's that' reaction, not a worksheet prompt. s6 question is the friend wanting to know if she should hand it over; closer answers BOTH the surface question AND the question of agency (it's mine now, by my mother's words).
  obligations_absorbed:  must-reinforce W00126/W00127/W00128/W00125/W00124 (これ/誰/ない/拾う/石) all carried — これ in s4, 誰 in s6 + closer, 拾う in s5, 石 throughout, ない in closer (「誰のでもないから」). must-introduce N5_nan_what (auto-tagged via 何 in s4) — reinforces N5_dare_who in s6 simultaneously.

FORBIDDEN THIS STORY:
  scene_class    (last 3): garden, room, study — picked classroom, fresh.
  anchor_object  (last 5): パン, 手紙, 石, 葉, 鳥 — anchor is 本, not the stone itself.
  closer_shape   (last 3): noun-TO-noun-WA-noun-WO-noun-noun, noun-WA-noun-MO-noun-MO-noun, noun-WA-noun-WO-noun-noun-P:ね-TO-noun — picked compound action+claim shape with から-clause logic.
  opening_seqs   (last 3): 夕方/私/部屋, もう/朝, 次/朝/私 — picked 教室/で/机.

NOTE on closer's reuse of 「誰のでもない」: this is intentional semantic INVERSION, not recitation. The mother said 'no one's' to mean 'unclaimed'; the narrator says 'no one's, so it's mine' — using the same words to flip the meaning from disclaim to claim. This is the story's literary point: the daughter has learned the legal-logic vocabulary from the mother and applies it to take possession. The recitation-vs-inversion judgment is a literary call, made deliberately.

sentences.jp: 教室で、机にかばんを置きました。
sentences.en: In the classroom, I put my bag on the desk.

sentences.jp: かばんから本を出して、開きました。
sentences.en: I took the book out of the bag and opened it.

sentences.jp: 本の中から石が落ちました。
sentences.en: From inside the book, a stone fell out.

sentences.jp: 隣の友達は石を見ました。
sentences.en: The friend at the next desk saw the stone.

sentences.jp: 「これは何ですか」と友達は聞きました。
私は石を拾って、友達に見せました。
sentences.en: I picked up the stone and showed it to my friend.

sentences.jp: 「誰の石ですか」と友達は聞きました。
sentences.en: "Whose stone is it?" my friend asked.

sentences.jp: 私は石を本に入れて、「誰のでもないから、私のだよ」と言いました。
sentences.en: I put the stone back in the book and said, "It belongs to no one — so it's mine."
